facts.text: The first modern Olympic Games were held in Athens, Greece, in 1896, featuring 241 athletes from 14 nations competing in 43 events.
Michael Phelps is the most decorated Olympian of all time with 28 medals, including 23 golds, earned across five Olympic Games.
The Olympic rings represent the five continents of Africa, the Americas, Asia, Europe, and Oceania, and every national flag contains at least one of the ring colors.
At the 1936 Berlin Olympics, Jesse Owens won four gold medals in track and field, defying Adolf Hitler's vision of Aryan supremacy.
The Paralympic Games originated in 1948 when Sir Ludwig Guttmann organized a sports competition for World War II veterans with spinal cord injuries at Stoke Mandeville, England.
Usain Bolt's lightning bolt celebration became one of the most iconic poses in Olympic history after his record-breaking 100m victory in Beijing 2008.
The first Winter Olympic Games were held in Chamonix, France, in 1924, with 258 athletes from 16 nations.
The Olympic motto was originally 'Citius, Altius, Fortius' (Faster, Higher, Stronger), and in 2021 the word 'Communiter' (Together) was added.
The Miracle on Ice at the 1980 Lake Placid Winter Olympics saw the underdog U.S. hockey team defeat the dominant Soviet Union 4-3 in one of the greatest upsets in sports history.
Skateboarding, surfing, sport climbing, and karate made their Olympic debuts at the Tokyo 2020 Games.
The IOC (International Olympic Committee) was founded on June 23, 1894, by Pierre de Coubertin, and this date is now celebrated as Olympic Day.
At the 1968 Mexico City Olympics, Tommie Smith and John Carlos raised black-gloved fists on the medal podium in a powerful protest for civil rights.
Larisa Latynina of the Soviet Union held the record for most Olympic medals (18) for 48 years before Michael Phelps surpassed her in 2012.
The Olympic flame relay tradition began at the 1936 Berlin Games, with the torch carried from Olympia, Greece, to the host city.
Russia was banned from competing under its own flag at the 2018 and 2022 Winter Olympics due to a state-sponsored doping scandal uncovered by WADA.
The 1992 Barcelona Olympics featured the Dream Team, the first U.S. men's basketball team composed of NBA professionals, including Michael Jordan, Magic Johnson, and Larry Bird.
Nadia Comaneci of Romania scored the first perfect 10 in Olympic gymnastics history at the 1976 Montreal Games, at just 14 years old.
The 2008 Beijing Olympics opening ceremony, directed by Zhang Yimou, is often considered the most spectacular in Olympic history, featuring 15,000 performers.
Breakdancing (breaking) made its Olympic debut at the Paris 2024 Games, becoming one of the newest sports added to the program.
The ancient Olympic Games were held in Olympia, Greece, from 776 BC to 393 AD, spanning over a millennium.
Paavo Nurmi, the Flying Finn, won nine gold medals in distance running across the 1920, 1924, and 1928 Olympics.
The Olympic Village concept was first introduced at the 1932 Los Angeles Games, providing housing for male athletes.
At the 2000 Sydney Olympics, Cathy Freeman lit the Olympic cauldron and later won gold in the 400m, becoming a symbol of reconciliation for Indigenous Australians.
The 1980 Moscow Olympics were boycotted by 66 countries, including the United States, in protest of the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan.
Carl Lewis won nine Olympic gold medals across four Games (1984–1996) in sprinting and long jump events.
The host city for the Olympics is selected by IOC members through a bidding process that can take years and cost millions of dollars.
Simone Biles has won seven Olympic medals in gymnastics, including four golds at Rio 2016, making her one of the most decorated gymnasts in history.
The 1972 Munich Olympics were marred by tragedy when 11 Israeli team members were taken hostage and killed by Palestinian terrorists.
Tug of war was an Olympic sport from 1900 to 1920, and croquet appeared only at the 1900 Paris Games.
The Olympic torch for the 1964 Tokyo Games was lit by Yoshinori Sakai, who was born in Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, the day the atomic bomb was dropped.
Los Angeles will host the 2028 Summer Olympics, becoming the third city to host the Games three times after London and Paris.
The first Olympic marathon was inspired by the legend of Pheidippides, who reportedly ran from Marathon to Athens to announce a Greek military victory.
At the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics, the Soviet Union and 13 allied nations staged a retaliatory boycott, mirroring the Western boycott of Moscow 1980.
South Korean archer Kim Soo-nyung won four gold medals across three Olympics (1988–2000), making her one of the greatest archers in Olympic history.
The Olympic rings were designed by Pierre de Coubertin in 1913 and first used at the 1920 Antwerp Games.
Swimmer Mark Spitz won seven gold medals at the 1972 Munich Olympics, a record that stood until Michael Phelps won eight golds in Beijing 2008.
The 2012 London Olympics opening ceremony, directed by Danny Boyle, celebrated British culture from the Industrial Revolution to the digital age.
Brisbane, Australia, will host the 2032 Summer Olympics, marking the third time Australia has hosted the Games.
At the 1896 Athens Olympics, winners received silver medals and an olive branch; gold medals were not awarded until 1904.
Fanny Blankers-Koen of the Netherlands won four gold medals at the 1948 London Olympics, earning the nickname the Flying Housewife.
The Olympic cauldron at the 1992 Barcelona Games was lit by Paralympic archer Antonio Rebollo, who shot a flaming arrow over the cauldron rim.
Golf was removed from the Olympics after 1904 and did not return until the 2016 Rio Games, a gap of 112 years.
The 1994 Lillehammer Winter Olympics marked a shift to a two-year stagger between Summer and Winter Games, which had previously been held in the same year.
Ethiopian runner Abebe Bikila won the 1960 Rome Olympic marathon running barefoot, becoming the first Black African to win an Olympic gold medal.
The Olympic flag was first flown at the 1920 Antwerp Olympics and is ceremonially passed from the outgoing host city to the incoming one at each closing ceremony.
Gymnast Kohei Uchimura of Japan won consecutive all-around gold medals at the 2012 and 2016 Olympics and is considered one of the greatest gymnasts ever.
The Paralympics have grown from 400 athletes at the 1960 Rome Games to over 4,400 athletes at the Tokyo 2020 Games.
At the 2014 Sochi Winter Olympics, Russia topped the medal count with 33 medals, but many were later stripped due to doping violations.
The modern pentathlon was created by Pierre de Coubertin and has been part of the Olympics since 1912, combining fencing, swimming, equestrian show jumping, shooting, and running.
Sonja Henie of Norway won three consecutive Olympic figure skating gold medals (1928, 1932, 1936) and later became a Hollywood film star.
The 2020 Tokyo Olympics were postponed to 2021 due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the first peacetime postponement in Olympic history.
Equestrian events are the only Olympic sports in which men and women compete directly against each other on equal terms.
Muhammad Ali (then Cassius Clay) won a gold medal in light heavyweight boxing at the 1960 Rome Olympics at age 18.
The IOC generates most of its revenue from broadcasting rights, which accounted for over 60% of total Olympic revenue in the 2013–2016 cycle.
Kerri Strug's vault on an injured ankle secured the first team gold for the U.S. women's gymnastics team at the 1996 Atlanta Olympics.
The Olympic flame is lit at the Temple of Hera in Olympia, Greece, using a parabolic mirror to focus sunlight, before beginning its relay to the host city.
At the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics, figure skater Nathan Chen of the USA won gold in the men's singles with a dominant free skate performance.
The marathon distance of 26.2 miles (42.195 km) was standardized at the 1908 London Olympics so the race could finish in front of the royal box.
Bob Beamon shattered the long jump world record at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics with a leap of 8.90 meters, a record that stood for 23 years.
Table tennis became an Olympic sport in 1988, and China has since dominated the event, winning the majority of gold medals.
Norwegian cross-country skier Marit Bjørgen is the most decorated Winter Olympian with 15 medals, including eight golds.
The 1996 Atlanta Olympics were marred by a bombing in Centennial Olympic Park that killed two people and injured over 100.
Olympic medals must be at least 60mm in diameter, 3mm thick, and the gold medal must contain at least 6 grams of pure gold plated over silver.
The 2024 Paris Olympics featured surfing events held in Tahiti, French Polynesia, over 15,000 km from the host city.
Swimmer Katie Ledecky has won seven Olympic gold medals and is considered one of the greatest female swimmers in history.
The abandoned Olympic venues in Athens from the 2004 Games became a cautionary example of the cost overruns and white elephants associated with hosting.
Eric Moussambani of Equatorial Guinea became famous at the 2000 Sydney Olympics for nearly drowning during his 100m freestyle heat, having only learned to swim months earlier.
The United States has won more Olympic medals than any other country, with over 2,600 total medals across Summer and Winter Games.
At the 1956 Melbourne Olympics, the People's Republic of China, Egypt, Iraq, and Lebanon boycotted due to various political disputes.
Wheelchair rugby, originally called murderball, became a full Paralympic medal sport in 2000 and is one of the most physical Paralympic events.
The 1936 Berlin Olympics were the first to be broadcast on television, though coverage was limited to venues in Berlin.
Jamaican sprinter Usain Bolt won eight Olympic gold medals across three Games (2008–2016), setting world records in the 100m, 200m, and 4x100m relay.
The concept of the Olympic Truce, or 'Ekecheiria,' originated in ancient Greece where wars were paused to allow safe travel to the Games.
At the 2016 Rio Olympics, the refugee team competed for the first time under the Olympic flag, featuring ten athletes from four countries.
Swedish shooter Oscar Swahn remains the oldest Olympic gold medalist, winning a team event at the 1912 Stockholm Games at age 64.
The 2028 Los Angeles Olympics will use existing venues and minimize new construction, aiming to be one of the most cost-effective Games in modern history.
Dick Fosbury revolutionized high jump at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics by going over the bar backwards, a technique now known as the Fosbury Flop.
Beach volleyball was added to the Olympic program at the 1996 Atlanta Games and quickly became one of the most popular spectator sports.
The Olympic oath, first introduced at the 1920 Antwerp Games, is recited by an athlete from the host country on behalf of all competitors.
Norway has won the most Winter Olympic medals of any country, with over 400 total medals, dominating sports like cross-country skiing and biathlon.
Indian field hockey dominated the Olympics from 1928 to 1956, winning six consecutive gold medals.
The Olympic Village at the London 2012 Games was converted into affordable housing after the event, providing over 2,800 homes.
Torvill and Dean's Bolero ice dance routine at the 1984 Sarajevo Winter Olympics earned unanimous perfect scores of 6.0 for artistic impression.
Baseball and softball were dropped from the Olympics after 2008, reinstated for Tokyo 2020, then dropped again for Paris 2024.
The first Olympic Games featured only male athletes; women first competed at the 1900 Paris Olympics in tennis and golf.
Ethiopian Haile Gebrselassie is a two-time Olympic gold medalist in the 10,000m (1996, 2000) and former marathon world record holder.
The 2014 Sochi Winter Olympics was the most expensive Olympics ever, costing an estimated $51 billion.
Hungarian swimmer Alfréd Hajós won two gold medals at the 1896 Athens Olympics, swimming in the open waters of the Bay of Zea in the Mediterranean Sea.
BMX racing became an Olympic sport at the 2008 Beijing Games, reflecting the IOC's efforts to include youth-oriented sports.
Tatyana McFadden, a wheelchair racer, has won 20 Paralympic medals across multiple Games, becoming one of the most prominent American Paralympians.
The 1948 London Olympics, known as the Austerity Games, were held on a shoestring budget just three years after World War II ended.
South Korea's short track speed skating team has won more Olympic medals in the sport than any other nation.
The volunteer program for the Olympics typically involves 40,000–70,000 people, making it one of the largest volunteer events in the world.
At the 1988 Seoul Olympics, Ben Johnson was stripped of his 100m gold medal after testing positive for the steroid stanozolol.
Greco-Roman wrestling has been part of every modern Olympic Games since 1896, making it one of the original Olympic sports.
The flame for the 1976 Montreal Olympics was transmitted via satellite from Athens to Ottawa in the form of an electronic signal.
At the 2004 Athens Olympics, the marathon followed the original route from Marathon to Athens, the same path legend attributes to Pheidippides.
Birgit Fischer of Germany won eight Olympic gold medals in canoeing across six Games (1980–2004), spanning 24 years.
The Olympic motto 'Citius, Altius, Fortius' was proposed by Henri Didon, a Dominican friar and friend of Pierre de Coubertin.
At the Paris 2024 Olympics, the opening ceremony was held along the River Seine rather than in a stadium, a first in Olympic history.
Competitors in ancient Greek Olympics performed nude, and married women were forbidden from watching under penalty of death.
China has dominated Olympic diving since the 1984 Los Angeles Games, winning more diving golds than any other nation.
The 1906 Intercalated Games in Athens are not officially recognized by the IOC despite being sanctioned at the time.
Swimmer Ian Thorpe of Australia, the Thorpedo, won five Olympic gold medals and was known for his size 17 feet.
Drug testing was first introduced at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics to combat performance-enhancing substance use.
French baron Pierre de Coubertin initiated the revival of the modern Olympics, inspired by archaeological excavations at ancient Olympia.
At the 2010 Vancouver Winter Olympics, Canada set a record for the most gold medals won by a host nation at a Winter Games with 14 golds.
The ancient Olympic Games originally featured only one event: the stadion, a footrace of about 192 meters.
Oscar Pistorius, a double amputee, competed in the 2012 London Olympics on carbon fiber blades, becoming the first amputee sprinter to compete at an Olympics.
The 1964 Tokyo Olympics were the first held in Asia and marked Japan's return to the world stage following World War II.
Rhythmic gymnastics became an Olympic sport in 1984, and Russia and the Soviet Union have historically dominated the discipline.
The cost of hosting the Olympics has steadily increased, with the average Summer Games costing over $10 billion by the 2020s.
Norwegian biathlete Ole Einar Bjørndalen won 13 Olympic medals, earning him the nickname King of Biathlon.
The Olympic Charter prohibits any kind of demonstration or political, religious, or racial propaganda in Olympic venues.
Yusra Mardini, a Syrian refugee who swam through the sea to escape her war-torn country, competed on the Olympic Refugee Team at Rio 2016 and Tokyo 2020.
The ancient Greek Olympic truce halted all conflicts to allow athletes and spectators to travel safely to Olympia.
At the 1924 Paris Olympics, Johnny Weissmuller won three gold medals in swimming before going on to fame as Tarzan in Hollywood films.
Climbing, which debuted at Tokyo 2020, combines three disciplines: lead climbing, bouldering, and speed climbing.
Romanian gymnast Nadia Comaneci's perfect scores were displayed as 1.00 on the scoreboard because the equipment could not display 10.00.
Since 1994, Winter and Summer Olympics have alternated every two years, ensuring an Olympic event occurs every other year.
The United States men's basketball team won seven consecutive gold medals from 1936 to 1968 before losing to the Soviet Union in 1972.
Sir Steve Redgrave of Great Britain won gold medals in rowing at five consecutive Olympics from 1984 to 2000.
At the 1960 Rome Games, Danish cyclist Knud Enemark Jensen collapsed and died during competition, prompting calls for drug testing reform.
The Olympic medal design changes for every Games, with the host city creating its own unique medal featuring the Olympic imagery on one side.
Tegla Loroupe of Kenya became a pioneer for women's distance running and served as chef de mission for the Refugee Olympic Team.
The 2012 London Olympics generated an estimated £9.9 billion in economic benefit for the United Kingdom.
Boxer Teófilo Stevenson of Cuba won three consecutive Olympic heavyweight gold medals (1972, 1976, 1980) and famously refused to turn professional.
The 1928 Amsterdam Olympics were the first to feature a permanent Olympic flame burning throughout the Games.
South Africa was banned from the Olympics from 1964 to 1988 due to its apartheid policies.
At the 2016 Rio Olympics, Mo Farah of Great Britain won the 5,000m and 10,000m double for a second consecutive Games.
The ancient Olympics included events like chariot racing, pankration (a mix of boxing and wrestling), and the pentathlon.
Esther Vergeer of the Netherlands won four consecutive Paralympic wheelchair tennis gold medals and retired with a 470-match winning streak.
The Tokyo 2020 Olympic medals were made from recycled electronics, including old smartphones and laptops donated by the Japanese public.
Florence Griffith Joyner, Flo-Jo, set the still-standing world records in the 100m and 200m at the 1988 Seoul Olympics.
The 1952 Helsinki Olympics were the first Games in which the Soviet Union competed, sparking an intense Cold War medal rivalry with the United States.
Women's boxing was added to the Olympic program at the 2012 London Games, completing the inclusion of women in all Olympic sports.
Pierre de Coubertin believed the Olympics should emphasize amateur athletics, a principle that gradually eroded until professionals were admitted in the 1990s.
At the 2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics, athletes from North and South Korea marched together under a unified flag during the opening ceremony.
Hungarian fencer Aladár Gerevich won gold medals in sabre at six consecutive Olympics from 1932 to 1960.
The Olympic rings colors—blue, yellow, black, green, and red on a white background—were chosen because every nation's flag contains at least one of these colors.
Snowboarding made its Olympic debut at the 1998 Nagano Winter Games, with giant slalom and halfpipe events.
At the 1904 St. Louis Olympics, the marathon was a near-disaster: the winner was disqualified for riding in a car, and another runner nearly died from rat poison and brandy given as a stimulant.
Germany (reunified) and South Korea have both used the Olympics as a platform for national reconciliation and diplomacy.
The 2032 Brisbane Olympics will be held across Southeast Queensland, utilizing existing and upgraded venues.
Eliud Kipchoge of Kenya, widely considered the greatest marathon runner, won Olympic marathon gold in 2016 and 2020.
The Olympic flame for the 2000 Sydney Games traveled underwater near the Great Barrier Reef, carried by a diver using a special flare.
Speed skating was included in the first Winter Olympics in 1924, and the Netherlands has historically dominated the sport.
Jim Thorpe won gold in the pentathlon and decathlon at the 1912 Stockholm Olympics but was stripped of his medals for having played semi-professional baseball; they were reinstated in 1983.
The 2026 Winter Olympics will be held in Milan-Cortina, Italy, returning the Winter Games to Italy for the first time since Turin 2006.
At every Olympic closing ceremony, the mayor of the host city passes the Olympic flag to the mayor of the next host city.
Wilma Rudolph overcame childhood polio to win three gold medals in sprinting at the 1960 Rome Olympics, becoming the fastest woman in the world.
The modern pentathlon was designed to simulate the experience of a 19th-century cavalry soldier behind enemy lines.
Para swimming has been part of every Paralympic Games since their inception in 1960 and features athletes across multiple disability classifications.
The 1932 Los Angeles Olympics saw the introduction of the victory podium and the three-medal system of gold, silver, and bronze.
At the 1976 Montreal Games, 22 African nations boycotted to protest New Zealand's rugby tour of apartheid South Africa.
The Olympic torch relay for the 2008 Beijing Games covered 137,000 km across five continents, the longest in Olympic history.
Cuban jumper Javier Sotomayor, the only person to high jump over 8 feet (2.45m), won gold at the 1992 Barcelona Olympics.
Triathlon was added to the Olympic program at the 2000 Sydney Games, combining swimming, cycling, and running.
The opening ceremony Parade of Nations always begins with Greece, the birthplace of the Olympics, and ends with the host nation.
At the 1908 London Olympics, Italian marathon runner Dorando Pietri was disqualified after officials helped him across the finish line when he collapsed from exhaustion.
Trampoline gymnastics became an Olympic event at the 2000 Sydney Games, with China dominating the discipline.
The 1998 Nagano Winter Olympics organizing committee controversially destroyed financial records, preventing a full audit of the Games' costs.
Allyson Felix holds the record for the most Olympic track and field medals by any American, with 11 medals including seven golds.
Archery was part of the early Olympics (1900–1920), dropped, and returned in 1972, with South Korea becoming the dominant force.
At the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, athlete protests were permitted for the first time, allowing gestures before and after events but not on the podium.
Swedish equestrian rider Henri de Saint Cyr won gold in dressage at both the 1952 and 1956 Olympics.
The 1920 Antwerp Olympics introduced the Olympic flag, oath, and the release of doves, all symbols that became lasting traditions.
Jamaican bobsled team's improbable appearance at the 1988 Calgary Winter Olympics inspired the movie Cool Runnings.
The first ever Olympic Winter Games featured only 16 events across six sports: bobsled, curling, ice hockey, skating, skiing, and military patrol.
China has won the most Olympic gold medals in weightlifting, table tennis, and diving combined.
At the 1912 Stockholm Olympics, Japan competed in the Olympics for the first time, sending just two athletes.
The IOC's Olympic Solidarity program provides financial assistance to National Olympic Committees in developing countries to support athlete training and participation.
Shooting events have been part of every modern Olympics except 1904 and 1928, making it one of the longest-running Olympic sports.
The first ever Winter Olympic gold medal was won by American speed skater Charles Jewtraw in the 500m event at Chamonix in 1924.
At the 2012 London Paralympics, South African runner Oscar Pistorius lost the 200m final to Brazilian Alan Oliveira and controversially accused him of using longer blades.
The original Olympic sports at Athens 1896 included athletics, cycling, fencing, gymnastics, shooting, swimming, tennis, weightlifting, and wrestling.
Lee Chong Wei of Malaysia won three consecutive Olympic silver medals in badminton (2008, 2012, 2016), becoming a national hero despite never winning gold.
Skeleton, where athletes race headfirst down an ice track, was discontinued after 1948 and returned to the Winter Olympics in 2002.
The 2024 Paris Olympics marked the first time an Olympics achieved gender parity, with equal numbers of male and female athletes.
Hicham El Guerrouj of Morocco won both the 1,500m and 5,000m at the 2004 Athens Olympics, a rare distance double.
London is the only city to have hosted the Summer Olympics three times: in 1908, 1948, and 2012.
At the 2008 Beijing Olympics, Jamaican relay teammate Nesta Carter tested positive for a banned substance years later, costing Usain Bolt one of his nine gold medals.
The International Paralympic Committee was founded in 1989, serving as the global governing body for the Paralympic Movement.
Water polo has been an Olympic sport since 1900, making it one of the earliest team sports in the modern Games.
Soviet gymnast Olga Korbut captivated audiences at the 1972 Munich Olympics with daring routines, popularizing gymnastics worldwide.
The 2028 LA Olympics will feature cricket and squash as new additions to the Olympic sports program.
Finnish runner Lasse Virén won the 5,000m and 10,000m double at two consecutive Olympics (1972, 1976), despite falling during the 10,000m final in Munich.
At the 1968 Mexico City Olympics, Bob Beamon's long jump record of 8.90m exceeded the limits of the optical measuring equipment and had to be measured manually.
The ancient Olympics featured only Greek male citizens and events lasted five days, including religious ceremonies honoring Zeus.
South Korea's dominance in archery is so complete that they have won more than 40 Olympic medals in the sport.
Judo was introduced as an Olympic sport at the 1964 Tokyo Olympics, the first Games held in Asia.
The Olympic torch for the 1996 Atlanta Games was lit by Muhammad Ali, who despite his trembling hands from Parkinson's disease, delivered an unforgettable moment.
Charlotte Dujardin of Great Britain won three Olympic dressage gold medals, becoming one of the most successful equestrians in Olympic history.
Polo was an Olympic sport from 1900 to 1936, with Argentina winning the last gold before it was removed from the program.
At the 2000 Sydney Paralympics, Spain's intellectually disabled basketball team was stripped of their gold medal after it was revealed most players did not have disabilities.
Handball has been a consistent Olympic sport since 1972, with European nations dominating the medal count.
The 1956 Melbourne Olympics were the first held in the Southern Hemisphere, with equestrian events held separately in Stockholm due to Australian quarantine laws.
American gymnast Mary Lou Retton became the first woman outside Eastern Europe to win the Olympic all-around gold medal at the 1984 Los Angeles Games.
The Olympic Hymn was composed by Spyridon Samaras in 1896 and officially adopted by the IOC in 1958.
Kenya has won more Olympic medals in middle and long-distance running than any other nation.
At the 1988 Seoul Olympics, diver Greg Louganis hit his head on the springboard during preliminaries but came back to win gold in both diving events.
Berlin 1936 was the first Olympics to use television broadcasting, electronic timing, and photo finishes for track events.
Freestyle skiing events like moguls and aerials were first introduced at the 1992 Albertville Winter Olympics.
The Olympic records page shows that the USA holds the all-time medal count lead with over 1,000 gold medals across all Games.
French fencer Laura Flessel-Colovic won five Olympic medals and carried the flag for France at the 2012 London opening ceremony.
At the 1936 Berlin Olympics, Korean marathon runner Sohn Kee-chung won gold but was forced to compete under the Japanese flag because Korea was under Japanese occupation.
The IOC introduced the Youth Olympic Games in 2010, held in Singapore, aimed at athletes aged 15 to 18.
Carl Lewis matched Jesse Owens's record of four track and field golds at a single Olympics at the 1984 Los Angeles Games.
Demonstration sports at past Olympics have included American football, glider flying, and roller hockey but never became permanent medal events.
At the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics, 15-year-old figure skater Kamila Valieva was at the center of a doping controversy that overshadowed the women's singles event.
Ethiopian Derartu Tulu and South African Elana Meyer embraced during a victory lap at the 1992 Barcelona Olympics, symbolizing post-apartheid unity.
Rowing has been on the Olympic program since 1900, though the 1896 rowing events were canceled due to bad weather.
New Zealand's Lisa Carrington has won five Olympic gold medals in canoe sprint, making her one of the country's most decorated Olympians.
The Refugee Olympic Team was formally established by the IOC in 2015 and has competed at every Summer Games since Rio 2016.
At the 1952 Helsinki Olympics, the Soviet Union made its Olympic debut and immediately challenged U.S. dominance, winning 71 medals.
The Olympic oath was updated in 2021 to include commitments against doping and discrimination alongside fair competition.
Rugby sevens was introduced at the 2016 Rio Olympics, with Fiji winning gold in a landmark moment for the Pacific island nation.
Dawn Fraser of Australia won the 100m freestyle swimming gold at three consecutive Olympics (1956, 1960, 1964).
The Paris 2024 Olympics used the Palace of Versailles as a venue for equestrian and modern pentathlon events.
At ancient Olympia, the statue of Zeus was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, standing 13 meters tall and made of ivory and gold.
South African swimmer Natalie du Toit competed in both the Olympics and Paralympics at Beijing 2008, finishing 16th in the open water 10km marathon swim.
Badminton has been an Olympic sport since 1992, with Asian nations—especially China, Indonesia, and South Korea—dominating the medal count.
American decathlete Rafer Johnson won gold at the 1960 Rome Olympics and later helped subdue Robert Kennedy's assassin in 1968.
Only five sports have appeared at every Summer Olympic Games: athletics, cycling, fencing, gymnastics, and swimming.
The 1984 Sarajevo Winter Olympics venues were later damaged and repurposed during the Bosnian War in the 1990s.
At the 2012 London Olympics, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Brunei sent female athletes for the first time, meaning all participating nations had women competitors.
Wrestler Aleksandr Karelin of Russia went undefeated for 13 years and won three consecutive Olympic golds (1988–1996) before a shocking final loss in 2000.
The Olympic Village at Sydney 2000 was noted for distributing 70,000 condoms to athletes, a number that has increased at subsequent Games.
Hungarian water polo team won nine Olympic gold medals, more than any other nation in the sport.
Mao Asada and Yuna Kim's rivalry defined women's figure skating at the 2010 Vancouver Winter Olympics, where Kim won gold.
Live pigeon shooting was an event at the 1900 Paris Olympics—the only time in Olympic history that animals were deliberately killed during competition.
Michael Johnson's 200m world record at the 1996 Atlanta Olympics, run in gold shoes, stood for 12 years.
At the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, Filipino weightlifter Hidilyn Diaz won the country's first-ever Olympic gold medal.
The 1924 Paris Olympics were featured in the film Chariots of Fire, which depicted the true stories of British runners Eric Liddell and Harold Abrahams.
Curling became a medal sport at the 1998 Nagano Winter Olympics after appearing as a demonstration sport in earlier Games.
Finnish javelin throwers won every Olympic gold in the event from 1908 to 1936, establishing Finland's legacy in the sport.
The 2006 Turin Winter Olympics saw Shani Davis become the first Black athlete to win an individual gold medal at the Winter Games.
At the 1900 Paris Olympics, events were spread over five months and many athletes didn't even know they were competing in the Olympics.
Cuban boxer Félix Savón won three consecutive Olympic heavyweight gold medals (1992, 2000, from three total Games).
The Olympic torch for the 2004 Athens Games visited every former Olympic host city during its relay.
Volleyball was added to the Olympic program at the 1964 Tokyo Games, with the Japanese women's team winning gold and earning the nickname Witches of the Orient.
At the 2016 Rio Olympics, Wayde van Niekerk of South Africa broke the 400m world record in lane 8, considered the worst lane for the event.
Taekwondo became a full Olympic medal sport at the 2000 Sydney Games after being a demonstration sport in 1988 and 1992.
The ancient Olympic Games were banned in 393 AD by Roman Emperor Theodosius I, who considered them pagan festivals.
Japan's Yuzuru Hanyu won back-to-back Olympic figure skating gold medals (2014, 2018) and became one of the most popular athletes in the sport's history.
American sprinter Allyson Felix competed in five consecutive Olympics (2004–2020), winning medals at each one.
The 1972 Munich Olympics introduced a colorful, playful design aesthetic created by Otl Aicher that influenced Olympic branding for decades.
At the 2008 Beijing Olympics, Usain Bolt won the 100m in 9.69 seconds while visibly slowing down and celebrating before the finish line.
Canadian ice hockey teams, both men's and women's, have historically dominated the Winter Olympics in the sport.
The 2020 Tokyo Olympics had no spectators in most venues due to COVID-19 restrictions, a first in Olympic history.
Daley Thompson of Great Britain won decathlon gold at both the 1980 and 1984 Olympics, scoring over 8,800 points each time.
At the 2024 Paris Olympics, the triathlon was nearly disrupted by concerns about pollution levels in the River Seine.
Synchronized swimming (now called artistic swimming) became an Olympic sport in 1984, with Russia and the Soviet Union winning the most medals.
Australian swimmer Shane Gould won three individual gold medals and set world records at age 15 at the 1972 Munich Olympics.
The IOC has 206 member countries, more than the United Nations, with associations like Olympic Council of Asia coordinating regional participation.
Chinese diver Fu Mingxia won her first Olympic gold medal at the 1992 Barcelona Games at just 13 years old.
At the 1988 Calgary Winter Olympics, Eddie the Eagle Edwards of Britain became a beloved underdog in ski jumping despite finishing last.
The 1936 Berlin Olympics were the first to introduce the torch relay from Olympia, carrying the flame through seven countries over 12 days.
Fencing is the only combat sport that has been featured at every modern Olympic Games since 1896.
At the 1996 Atlanta Olympics, Donovan Bailey of Canada won the 100m, ending a controversy over who was the world's fastest man.
The Paralympic classification system groups athletes by functional ability to ensure fair competition across various physical and intellectual disabilities.
Weightlifting has been an Olympic sport since 1896 but was absent from the 1900, 1908, and 1912 Games.
The 2000 Sydney Olympics introduced the first online ticketing system, with over 87% of tickets purchased through the internet.
Czech long-distance runner Emil Zátopek won the 5,000m, 10,000m, and marathon at the 1952 Helsinki Olympics—the marathon being his first attempt at the distance.
At the 2004 Athens Olympics, the Greek women's water polo team won silver, sparking a national celebration in the sport's country of origin.
The Olympic cauldron at the 2000 Sydney Games was lit amid a ring of fire and water, creating a dramatic visual spectacle.
Table tennis has been dominated by China since its Olympic introduction, with Chinese athletes winning 32 of 37 gold medals through the 2024 Games.
Ethiopian marathon legend Abebe Bikila won his second consecutive Olympic marathon gold at the 1964 Tokyo Games, this time wearing shoes.
The first Olympic mascot was Waldi, a dachshund, created for the 1972 Munich Olympics.
At the 1924 Paris Games, swimmer Gertrude Ederle won bronze before becoming the first woman to swim the English Channel two years later.
The IOC awards the Olympic Order to individuals who have made outstanding contributions to the Olympic Movement.
Kayak and canoe events have been part of the Olympics since 1936, with Hungary and Germany among the top nations.
At the 2012 London Olympics, Usain Bolt became the first sprinter to successfully defend both the 100m and 200m titles.
The ancient Olympics' most prestigious event was the stadion race, approximately 192 meters, equivalent to the length of the stadium in Olympia.
Mountain biking was added to the Olympic program at the 1996 Atlanta Games, with cross-country being the featured discipline.
At the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics, mixed-team events were expanded, including a new mixed team relay in short track speed skating.
Kenya's Kipchoge Keino is considered the father of African distance running, winning gold in the 1,500m at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics.
The 1960 Rome Olympics were the first Games to be widely broadcast on television, reaching millions of viewers worldwide.
At the 2016 Rio Olympics, long jumper Jeff Henderson of the USA won gold on his final attempt, leaping 8.38 meters.
Sailing has been an Olympic sport since 1900, though it was canceled at the 1896 Athens Games due to lack of wind and rough seas.
The 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics made Beijing the first city to host both a Summer (2008) and Winter Olympics.
American swimmer Jenny Thompson won the most Olympic medals by any female swimmer with 12, including eight golds, all in relay events.
At the 1936 Berlin Olympics, the Japanese pole vault event resulted in a tied silver medal that competitors resolved by cutting their medals in half and fusing them together.
The Olympic Solidarity Commission distributes broadcasting revenue to help athletes from developing nations train and compete.
At the 1992 Barcelona Games, Vitaly Scherbo of Belarus won six Olympic gold medals in gymnastics, the most in a single Games for a male gymnast.
Lacrosse appeared as a medal sport at the 1904 and 1908 Olympics and returned as a demonstration sport in 1928 and 1948.
Shooter Kim Rhode of the USA won medals in six consecutive Olympics (1996–2016), the first American to achieve this feat in an individual sport.
At the 2000 Sydney Olympics, British rower Steve Redgrave completed an unprecedented five consecutive gold medals spanning 1984 to 2000.
The 2028 LA Olympics will use the Intuit Dome, SoFi Stadium, and the LA Memorial Coliseum, which hosted the 1932 and 1984 opening ceremonies.
Pakistan has won four Olympic field hockey gold medals, all between 1960 and 1984.
The first Winter Paralympics were held in Örnsköldsvik, Sweden, in 1976, featuring alpine and cross-country skiing for amputees and visually impaired athletes.
African Americans were excluded from many early Olympic trials, and it wasn't until 1936 that a significant number competed, led by Jesse Owens.
At the 1968 Grenoble Winter Olympics, Jean-Claude Killy of France won all three alpine skiing gold medals.
Artistic gymnastics at the Olympics features six apparatus for men and four for women, with the all-around combining all events.
Ethiopian runner Tirunesh Dibaba won three Olympic gold medals in long-distance running (2008, 2012), earning the nickname Baby Faced Destroyer.
The 1908 London Olympics established the current marathon distance of 26 miles and 385 yards, adjusted to accommodate the British royal family.
Modern Olympic gold medals are actually made of sterling silver coated with at least 6 grams of gold.
At the 2004 Athens Olympics, Italian Alex Schwazer won the 50km race walk but was later caught doping before the 2012 Games.
The Parade of Nations at the opening ceremony uses the alphabetical order of the host country's language.
Wrestler Kaori Icho of Japan won four consecutive Olympic gold medals (2004–2016) in women's freestyle wrestling.
The 2028 Los Angeles Olympics will mark 100 years since the first Winter Olympics were held in Chamonix in 1924.
At the 2016 Rio Olympics, PV Sindhu of India won silver in badminton, inspiring millions across the country.
The 1992 Albertville Winter Olympics introduced short track speed skating as a medal sport.
The ancient Olympics awarded olive wreaths to victors, sourced from the sacred wild olive tree near the Temple of Zeus.
At the 1948 London Olympics, Fanny Blankers-Koen chose not to enter the long jump and high jump, events in which she held world records, because of scheduling conflicts.
North Korea and South Korea have periodically fielded joint teams, including at the 2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics women's ice hockey tournament.
Canoe slalom, where paddlers navigate through gates on whitewater rapids, became an Olympic sport in 1972, was removed, and returned permanently in 1992.
Michael Phelps won his 23rd Olympic gold medal in his final career race, the 4x100m medley relay at the 2016 Rio Games.
The Olympic Program Commission meets regularly to evaluate which sports should be added or removed based on global popularity and other criteria.
At the 2014 Sochi Olympics, Dutch speed skaters won 23 of 36 available medals, including eight gold, completely dominating the sport.
Swedish thrower Parry O'Brien revolutionized the shot put technique and won two Olympic golds (1952, 1956) with his signature glide technique.
The IOC's Rule 50 historically banned political demonstrations on the podium; the rule was relaxed for Tokyo 2020 to allow gestures before events.
At the 2024 Paris Olympics, Noah Lyles of the USA won the 100m final by five thousandths of a second, the closest Olympic 100m finish ever.
Japanese judoka Yasuhiro Yamashita won gold at the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics despite suffering a torn calf muscle during the competition.
The 1896 Athens Olympics had no standard uniform rules; many athletes competed in their everyday clothes.
Sprinter Marion Jones was stripped of all five medals won at the 2000 Sydney Olympics after admitting to steroid use in 2007.
At the Athens 2004 Olympics, Liu Xiang of China won the 110m hurdles, becoming the first Chinese man to win an Olympic gold in a track event.
Ice hockey has been part of the Olympic program since 1920 (summer) and 1924 (winter), making it one of the oldest Winter Olympic sports.
The Paralympic symbol of three agitos (meaning 'I move' in Latin) was adopted in 2003, representing motion and the worldwide reach of the movement.
At the 2008 Beijing Olympics, jamaican sprinter Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce won the 100m gold, beginning her decade-long dominance in women's sprinting.
The IOC requires host cities to provide anti-doping laboratories that can process thousands of samples during the Games.
Wrestling is considered one of the oldest competitive sports and has been part of the Olympics since the ancient Games.
At the 2010 Vancouver Winter Olympics, Georgian luger Nodar Kumaritashvili was killed during a training run just hours before the opening ceremony, prompting safety reviews.
Japanese swimmer Kosuke Kitajima won back-to-back Olympic gold medals in both the 100m and 200m breaststroke (2004, 2008).
The Olympic bid process has become increasingly competitive, with cities spending up to $150 million on their campaigns.
Artistic gymnastics replaced the traditional compulsory exercises with an all-optional format starting at the 2000 Sydney Olympics.
At the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics, American Carl Lewis won four gold medals in the same events Jesse Owens had won at the 1936 Berlin Games.
The IOC generates over $7 billion per Olympic cycle from broadcasting rights, sponsorships, and licensing agreements.
At the 1980 Moscow Olympics, British runners Sebastian Coe and Steve Ovett waged an epic rivalry, each winning gold in the other's preferred event.
Mixed martial arts has never been an Olympic sport, though discussions about its potential inclusion have occurred in recent years.
The 1912 Stockholm Olympics introduced electronic timing and public address systems, pioneering modern sports technology.
At the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, Australian swimmer Emma McKeon won seven medals (four gold, three bronze), the most by a female athlete at a single Games.
The first Olympic Winter Games in 1924 were originally called International Winter Sports Week and were retroactively designated as the first Winter Olympics by the IOC.
Kenyan runner David Rudisha's 800m victory at the 2012 London Olympics, in a world record time of 1:40.91, is widely regarded as one of the greatest Olympic races ever.
The Olympic Movement spreads across 206 National Olympic Committees, each responsible for their country's Olympic team.
At the 2000 Sydney Olympics, Australian sprinter Cathy Freeman wore a full-body swift suit, sparking debate about athletic wear innovation.
Golf featured at the 1900 and 1904 Olympics before its 112-year absence; it returned as a permanent sport in 2016.
Italian sprinter Marcell Jacobs shocked the world by winning the 100m gold at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, becoming the first European champion in the event since 1992.
At the 1972 Munich Olympics, the basketball final between the USA and Soviet Union ended in controversy when the clock was reset multiple times, leading to a disputed Soviet victory.
Sport climbing at the Olympics combines three disciplines, and in 2024 separated speed climbing into its own event due to athlete complaints.
The 2012 London Olympics used HMS Belfast and the Tower of London as backdrop settings for beach volleyball, blending historic landmarks with modern sports.
Moroccan runner Hicham El Guerrouj holds the world record for the mile (3:43.13) and won Olympic gold in the 1,500m and 5,000m at Athens 2004.
Only Greece, Australia, France, Great Britain, and Switzerland have sent athletes to every Summer Olympic Games since 1896.
At the 2024 Paris Olympics, the Place de la Concorde served as an urban sports venue for skateboarding, BMX, and breakdancing.
Cross-country skiing is the oldest Winter Olympic sport, with roots in Scandinavian military and transportation history.
At the 1968 Mexico City Olympics, long jumper Bob Beamon was so stunned by his own record-breaking leap that he suffered a cataplectic seizure on the field.
Pierre de Coubertin modeled the modern Olympics after the English public school sports tradition and ancient Greek athletic ideals.
The Olympic Winter Games have never been held in the Southern Hemisphere due to the reversal of seasons.
Czech midfielder and former Ballon d'Or winner Pavel Nedvěd competed in Olympic football; the sport has been part of the Games since 1900.
The 1996 Atlanta Olympics were the first Summer Games to feature women's football (soccer), with the USA winning the inaugural gold medal.
At the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, Suni Lee won the women's gymnastics all-around title after Simone Biles withdrew citing mental health concerns, sparking important conversations about athlete well-being.
The Winter Paralympics feature six sports: alpine skiing, biathlon, cross-country skiing, ice hockey, snowboard, and wheelchair curling.
Bob Mathias won the Olympic decathlon at the 1948 London Games at age 17, making him the youngest male Olympic athletics gold medalist at the time.
At the 1904 St. Louis Olympics, future Tarzan star Johnny Weissmuller was not yet born—those Games featured bizarre events including mud fighting.
The 2032 Brisbane Olympics bid was the first awarded under the IOC's new Future Host Commission process, which replaced the traditional bid competition.
Tessa Virtue and Scott Moir of Canada won the ice dance gold at the 2010 and 2018 Winter Olympics, becoming the most decorated ice dancers in Olympic history.
The ancient Olympics were held every four years, a period known as an Olympiad, which was used as a unit of time in ancient Greece.
Swimmer Alexander Popov of Russia won consecutive Olympic 50m and 100m freestyle gold medals in 1992 and 1996 despite being stabbed in a street attack between Games.
The 1976 Montreal Olympics left the city with a debt that took 30 years to pay off, becoming a cautionary tale about Olympic cost overruns.
At the 2016 Rio Olympics, the Refugee Olympic Team received a standing ovation during the opening ceremony's Parade of Nations.
Simone Biles withdrew from multiple events at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics due to the twisties, a dangerous mental block affecting spatial awareness in gymnastics.
The 2000 Sydney Olympics are often cited as one of the best-organized Games in modern history.
At the 1920 Antwerp Olympics, American figure skater Theresa Weld was warned by judges for performing jumps they deemed unfeminine.
The youngest known Olympic champion was Greek gymnast Dimitrios Loundras, who won bronze at age 10 in the 1896 Athens Games.
Biathlon combines cross-country skiing and rifle shooting and has been part of the Winter Olympics since 1960.
At the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics, the Games turned a profit of over $200 million, proving that hosting could be financially viable.
The Olympic Village at the Tokyo 2020 Games featured cardboard beds that became a viral topic on social media when athletes tested their durability.
Ethiopian marathon runner Mamo Wolde won gold at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics and silver at the 1972 Munich Games.
Fencer Valentina Vezzali of Italy won six Olympic medals, including three consecutive individual foil golds (2000, 2004, 2008).
The IOC's TOP (The Olympic Partners) program features major global sponsors like Coca-Cola, Visa, Samsung, and Toyota.
At the 2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics, Norway topped the medal table with 39 medals, including 14 golds.
The 1100-meter Olympic marathon swimming event (also called open water 10km) was added at the 2008 Beijing Olympics.
American gymnast Simone Biles returned from her Tokyo withdrawal to win bronze on the balance beam, demonstrating remarkable resilience.
Skeleton racing involves athletes sliding headfirst down an icy track on a small sled at speeds of up to 130 km/h.
At the 2012 London Olympics, 10,568 athletes from 204 nations competed in 302 events across 26 sports.
The 1940 and 1944 Olympics were canceled due to World War II; Tokyo and Helsinki had been chosen as host cities.
South Korean speed skater Lee Sang-hwa won consecutive 500m gold medals at the 2010 and 2014 Winter Olympics.
Skateboard legend Tony Hawk never competed in the Olympics because skateboarding was not included until after his competitive career.
At the 1992 Barcelona Olympics, the Dream Team defeated their opponents by an average margin of 44 points per game.
The Olympic torch for the 1956 Melbourne Games was carried on horseback across part of Australia due to the vastness of the country.
Ethiopian runner Haile Gebrselassie's racing style, with one arm held closer to his body than the other, was attributed to his childhood habit of running to school carrying his books.
The 2024 Paris Olympics used the Grand Palais for fencing and taekwondo, turning the historic exhibition hall into a sports venue.
At the 1896 Athens Olympics, the marathon was won by Spiridon Louis, a Greek water carrier, who became a national hero.
Women's marathon was not added to the Olympic program until the 1984 Los Angeles Games, where Joan Benoit of the USA won gold.
The 1998 Nagano Winter Olympics saw women's ice hockey debut as an Olympic sport, with the USA winning the first gold medal.
At the 2016 Rio Olympics, host country Brazil won their first ever Olympic gold in men's football, with Neymar scoring the decisive penalty.
The Olympic Agenda 2020, adopted in 2014, introduced 40 recommendations to make the Games more sustainable and affordable.
American diver Pat McCormick won double gold medals (platform and springboard) at both the 1952 and 1956 Olympics.
The 1928 Amsterdam Olympics were the first to include women's athletics and gymnastics events.
At the 2014 Sochi Winter Olympics, Meryl Davis and Charlie White won the first Olympic ice dance gold medal for the United States.
The 3x3 basketball format made its Olympic debut at the 2020 Tokyo Games, reflecting the sport's global street culture.
At the 2012 London Olympics, the opening ceremony featured Queen Elizabeth II appearing in a James Bond-inspired parachute entrance.
The Salt Lake City 2002 Winter Olympics were tainted by a bid scandal in which IOC members were found to have accepted bribes from the organizing committee.
At the 1996 Atlanta Olympics, Marie-José Pérec of France won the 200m and 400m double, while Michael Johnson did the same for the men.
The Paralympics derive their name from the Greek preposition 'para' meaning alongside, signifying that they run parallel to the Olympics.
North Korea has won 16 Olympic gold medals, with most coming in weightlifting, wrestling, and shooting.
At the 2023 IOC session, India won the rights to host the 2036 Olympics, pending a future announcement.
Swedish discus thrower Adolfo Consolini won the Olympic title in 1948 and competed in four consecutive Olympics spanning 16 years.
The 1924 Chamonix Winter Olympics featured only six sports compared to the 15 at the 2022 Beijing Winter Games.
At the 1936 Berlin Olympics, Helene Mayer, a Jewish fencer, competed for Germany and won a silver medal, giving a Nazi salute on the podium under duress.
The 2008 Beijing Olympics featured a Bird's Nest stadium designed by architects Herzog & de Meuron, which became an icon of modern architecture.
At the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, Caeleb Dressel of the USA won five gold medals in swimming, solidifying his status as the world's fastest sprinter in the pool.
Track and field legend Jackie Joyner-Kersee won three Olympic gold medals and is considered one of the greatest all-around female athletes in history.
The Olympic Charter states that the Olympic Games are competitions between athletes, not between countries, though unofficial medal tallies dominate media coverage.
At the 2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics, 17-year-old American snowboarder Chloe Kim won gold in the halfpipe with back-to-back 1080-degree spins.
Pierre de Coubertin initially opposed women's participation in the Olympics, believing athletics were too strenuous for them.
At the 2024 Paris Paralympics, over 4,400 athletes competed in 549 events across 22 sports.
The 1972 Munich Olympics introduced Waldi the dachshund as the first official mascot, starting a long tradition of Olympic mascots.
Jamaica's 4x100m relay team of Bolt, Blake, Carter, and Frater set a still-standing world record of 36.84 seconds at the 2012 London Olympics.
